category_summary:
**Anthropic** dominated the day's discourse with two major stories. **Boris Cherny** [announced Claude subscriptions](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-ef0422703799) will no longer cover third-party tool usage (2.1M views), framing it as sustainable capacity management. The community debated the impact on the **OpenClaw** ecosystem, with **Nathan Lambert** noting it was already existing policy. **Anthropic** [offered credits, refunds](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-ae04fb2a7fce), and discounted bundles as remediation.

- **Anthropic** also [unveiled 'model diffing'](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-28ab0ca40dd7) research comparing open-weight AI models, revealing a ['CCP alignment' feature](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-0ee6df3c275b) in **Qwen** and 'American exceptionalism' in **Llama** — a striking geopolitical interpretability finding
- **Ethan Mollick** highlighted two important studies: an [independent extension](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-67307596f8f8) of **METR**'s time-horizon analysis showing a 5.7-month doubling time for AI cybersecurity capabilities, and a **Nature** [paper revealing](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-91f9a2d74307) that AI diagnostic capability doesn't translate to real-world usability
- **Mollick** also [declared the RAG era](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-c377d102f7c1) effectively over as the dominant paradigm, sparking wide debate
- **Levelsio** made a [notable reversal](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-2dc6f30db494), admitting vibe coding into production is dangerous after encountering security issues (377K views, 1.5K likes)
- **Google**'s **Gemma 4** launch drew [detailed analysis](/?date=2026-04-04&category=social#item-08b7c669870d) from **Nathan Lambert**, who argued open model success depends more on finetunability and tooling than benchmarks
category_summary_html:
Anthropic dominated the day's discourse with two major stories. Boris Cherny announced Claude subscriptions will no longer cover third-party tool usage (2.1M views), framing it as sustainable capacity management. The community debated the impact on the OpenClaw ecosystem, with Nathan Lambert noting it was already existing policy. Anthropic offered credits, refunds, and discounted bundles as remediation.

  • Anthropic also unveiled 'model diffing' research comparing open-weight AI models, revealing a 'CCP alignment' feature in Qwen and 'American exceptionalism' in Llama — a striking geopolitical interpretability finding
  • Ethan Mollick highlighted two important studies: an independent extension of METR's time-horizon analysis showing a 5.7-month doubling time for AI cybersecurity capabilities, and a Nature paper revealing that AI diagnostic capability doesn't translate to real-world usability
  • Mollick also declared the RAG era effectively over as the dominant paradigm, sparking wide debate
  • Levelsio made a notable reversal, admitting vibe coding into production is dangerous after encountering security issues (377K views, 1.5K likes)
  • Google's Gemma 4 launch drew detailed analysis from Nathan Lambert, who argued open model success depends more on finetunability and tooling than benchmarks

themes.name: Anthropic Third-Party Tool Restriction
themes.description: Anthropic (via Boris Cherny) announced that Claude subscriptions will no longer cover usage on third-party tools like OpenClaw. This was the dominant story in this batch, with massive engagement (2.1M views on the main announcement). Anthropic framed it as enforcing existing policy, managing capacity sustainably, and optimizing for subscription workload patterns. They offered refunds, credits, and discounted usage bundles. Nathan Lambert validated it as a smart strategic move.

themes.name: Anthropic Model Diffing Research
themes.description: Anthropic introduces 'model diffing' to compare AI models like software code diffs, revealing unique features per model. Found 'CCP alignment' in Qwen and 'American exceptionalism' in Llama - a significant interpretability and safety finding.

themes.name: AI Capabilities & Safety Measurement
themes.description: Independent extension of METR's time-horizon analysis to cybersecurity shows 5.7 month doubling time. Frontier models now match human experts at 10.5-hour tasks. Quantitative evidence of accelerating AI capabilities.

themes.name: AI Capability Scaling & Safety
themes.description: METR time-horizon analysis extended to offensive cybersecurity showing consistent 5.7-month doubling of AI task complexity, with frontier models now matching human expert performance on multi-hour tasks.

themes.name: Gemma 4 Launch & Open Source AI
themes.description: Google released Gemma 4 with Apache 2.0 licensing, claiming it outperforms models 10-20x its size. HuggingFace CEO frames it as Google reclaiming the open-source crown from Chinese AI. Practical local deployment instructions shared.

themes.name: Vibe Coding Security & Risks
themes.description: Levelsio's high-profile admission that vibe coding in production is dangerous, combined with Santiago's analysis of AI code generating exponential technical debt. A pivotal moment for the vibe coding movement.

themes.name: RAG Paradigm Shift
themes.description: Emollick declares the RAG era is 'short-lived' - while still useful, it's no longer the dominant paradigm for supplying context to agents, suggesting long-context windows and other approaches are taking over.

themes.name: Open Model Ecosystem & Gemma 4
themes.description: Nathan Lambert's analysis arguing open model success depends on tooling and finetunability rather than benchmarks, with Gemma 4 as the focal point. Qwen highlighted as excelling where Gemma has struggled. Calls for research on what makes models finetunable.

themes.name: Keras Ecosystem & Tooling
themes.description: Major announcements from Chollet: Keras Kinetic (cloud TPU/GPU via decorators), Gemma 4 on KerasHub, Keras 3.14 preview, FunctionGemma, CLAHE preprocessing layer.

themes.name: Agent Memory Systems
themes.description: Multiple prominent figures (Harrison Chase, Matt Shumer) discussing the immaturity of agent memory. Chase argues memory must be in open harnesses, not proprietary APIs. Shumer soliciting community recommendations suggests no clear winner exists.

items.title: @jaredctate We're big fans of open source. I actually just put up a few PRs to improve prompt cache ...
items.content:
@jaredctate We're big fans of open source. I actually just put up a few PRs to improve prompt cache efficiency for OpenClaw specifically.

This is more about engineering constraints. Our systems are highly optimized for one kind of workload, and to serve as many people as possible with the most intelligent models, we are continuing to optimize that. 

When you use an API key or overages it should still work. The issue was just subs.

If you still want to cancel, we're giving full refunds. We know not everyone realized this isn't something we support, and this is an attempt to make it clear and explicit.
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: Cherny provides detailed response: Anthropic supports open source (submitted PRs to improve OpenClaw prompt cache efficiency), but engineering constraints require optimized subscription workloads. API/overages still work; issue is subscription-level optimization.
items.reasoning: Most detailed technical explanation of the policy change, 136K views. Reveals Anthropic's nuanced position - supportive of open source but constrained by engineering tradeoffs.
items.summary_html: Cherny provides detailed response: Anthropic supports open source (submitted PRs to improve OpenClaw prompt cache efficiency), but engineering constraints require optimized subscription workloads. API/overages still work; issue is subscription-level optimization.
items.content_html:
@jaredctate We're big fans of open source. I actually just put up a few PRs to improve prompt cache efficiency for OpenClaw specifically.

This is more about engineering constraints. Our systems are highly optimized for one kind of workload, and to serve as many people as possible with the most intelligent models, we are continuing to optimize that.

When you use an API key or overages it should still work. The issue was just subs.

If you still want to cancel, we're giving full refunds. We know not everyone realized this isn't something we support, and this is an attempt to make it clear and explicit.

items.title: @ashen_one I know it sucks. Fundamentally engineering is about tradeoffs, and one of the things we d...
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: Cherny explains to OpenClaw community that subscription systems are optimized for specific usage patterns; third-party services aren't optimized the same way, making support unsustainable. He personally submitted PRs to improve prompt cache hit rates for OpenClaw.
items.reasoning: 84K views. Adds important color about sustainability concerns and Anthropic's goodwill efforts toward open source tools even while restricting subscription access.
items.summary_html: Cherny explains to OpenClaw community that subscription systems are optimized for specific usage patterns; third-party services aren't optimized the same way, making support unsustainable. He personally submitted PRs to improve prompt cache hit rates for OpenClaw.
items.content_html:
@ashen_one I know it sucks. Fundamentally engineering is about tradeoffs, and one of the things we do to serve a lot of customers is optimize the way subscriptions work to serve as many people as possible with the best model.

Third party services are not optimized in this way, so it's really hard for us to do sustainably.

I did put up a few PRs to improve prompt cache hit rate for OpenClaw in particular, which should help for folks using it with Claude via API/overages.

items.title: It’s 11th year and counting! Teaching the first lecture of @cs231n every year has been a highlight o...
items.content: It’s 11th year and counting! Teaching the first lecture of @cs231n every year has been a highlight of my spring seasons. As usual, I asked students which departments or schools they come from @Stanford . Increasingly, students raise their hands to indicate that they come from all seven schools on campus, from @StanfordEng to @StanfordMed @StanfordHumSci @StanfordGSB @StanfordLaw @StanfordEd @stanforddoerr . AI is truly a horizontal technology that excites students across all backgrounds and disciplines!🤩
items.author: @drfeifei
items.summary: Fei-Fei Li celebrates 11th year teaching CS231N at Stanford, noting students now come from all seven schools including law, medicine, business, and humanities, calling AI a 'horizontal technology.'
items.reasoning: Highly credible author (pioneer of ImageNet, Stanford professor). Significant cultural observation about AI's cross-disciplinary reach. Highest engagement in batch (862 likes, 46K views). Speaks to AI's mainstreaming across academia.
items.summary_html: Fei-Fei Li celebrates 11th year teaching CS231N at Stanford, noting students now come from all seven schools including law, medicine, business, and humanities, calling AI a 'horizontal technology.'

items.title: Gemma 4 looks great on paper, just what many people need, but mostly I'm focused on the questions ar...
items.content:
Gemma 4 looks great on paper, just what many people need, but mostly I'm focused on the questions around what makes an open model succeed in the long term. 

We desperately need to build a field of research on "what makes a finetunable model" if we want the open model economy to succeed. Too much of that research happens behind closed doors of companies.

Plus, 240 days after The ATOM Project was released, I share reflections on the recovery of American models, and what that means for the next phase.

https://t.co/Kiex6catdp
items.author: @natolambert
items.summary: Following up on his [Social](/?date=2026-04-03&category=social#item-28826f66dc18) commentary yesterday, Nathan Lambert's detailed blog post analysis of Gemma 4, arguing the open model ecosystem needs more research on 'what makes a finetunable model'. Reflects on ATOM Project 240 days later and recovery of American open models.
items.reasoning: Substantive analysis from one of the most thoughtful voices on open models. High engagement (191 likes). Raises important strategic questions about open model economics and finetunability research. ATOM Project update adds policy dimension.

Gemma 4 looks great on paper, just what many people need, but mostly I'm focused on the questions around what makes an open model succeed in the long term.

We desperately need to build a field of research on "what makes a finetunable model" if we want the open model economy to succeed. Too much of that research happens behind closed doors of companies.

Plus, 240 days after The ATOM Project was released, I share reflections on the recovery of American models, and what that means for the next phase.

items.title:
Google just re-entered the game 🔥🔥

They want to take the crown 👑 back from Chinese open source AI.
...
items.content:
Google just re-entered the game 🔥🔥

They want to take the crown 👑 back from Chinese open source AI.

And... Gemma 4 is FINALLY Apache 2.0 aka real-open-source-licensed.

From what I've seen it's going to be a pretty significant model. But give it a try yourself today:

brew install llama.cpp --HEAD

if you have at least 24GB of RAM or VRAM, run the (very good) 26B MOE:

llama-server -hf ggml-org/gemma-4-26B-A4B-it-GGUF:Q4_K_M

if you have 16GB of RAM or VRAM, run the dense E4B:

llama-server -hf ggml-org/gemma-4-E4B-it-GGUF:Q8_0
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement Delangue provides detailed instructions for running Gemma 4 locally, celebrating that it's finally Apache 2.0 licensed. Notes Google is trying to reclaim the open-source AI crown from Chinese models.

items.title:
Here’s everything we launched this week (we promise not a single one of these is a joke):

— Gemma 4...
items.content:
Here’s everything we launched this week (we promise not a single one of these is a joke):

— Gemma 4, bringing our most intelligent open models and breakthrough reasoning to your personal hardware and devices while outcompeting models 20x its size

— Veo 3.1 Lite, our latest video generation model, which delivers the same speed as Veo 3.1 Fast but at half the cost

— Two new service tiers in the Gemini API in @GoogleAIStudio, bringing you granular control over cost and reliability through a single, unified interface 

— Focus mode in @GoogleAIStudio, the fastest way to make targeted edits to specific parts of your apps 

— New AI features launched to Google Vids from @GoogleWorkspace, including high-quality video generation from Veo 3.1, available to all users at no cost
items.author: @GoogleAI

— Veo 3.1 Lite, our latest video generation model, which delivers the same speed as Veo 3.1 Fast but at half the cost

— Two new service tiers in the Gemini API in @GoogleAIStudio, bringing you granular control over cost and reliability through a single, unified interface

— Focus mode in @GoogleAIStudio, the fastest way to make targeted edits to specific parts of your apps

items.title: People are too obsessed with benchmarks for open models. The core determining factor of success ofte...
items.content:
People are too obsessed with benchmarks for open models. The core determining factor of success often is:
1. Immediate & long term tooling support.
2. Finetunability

Tbh Gemma has struggled here in the past. Qwen has excelled at it. It's where the winners are crowned. https://t.co/LkGb3tHjA3

items.title: Perhaps the craziest thing that was introduced on the Keras community call today: Keras Kinetic, a n...
items.content:
Perhaps the craziest thing that was introduced on the Keras community call today: Keras Kinetic, a new library that lets you run jobs on cloud TPU/GPU via a simple decorator -- like Modal but with TPU support.

When you call a decorated function, Kinetic handles the entire remote execution pipeline:

- Packages your function, local code, and data dependencies
- Builds a container with your dependencies via Cloud Build (cached after first build)
- Runs the job on a GKE cluster with the requested accelerator (TPU or GPU)
- Returns the result to your local machine (logs are streamed in real time, and the function's return value is delivered back as if it ran locally)
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet announces Keras Kinetic: a new library for running jobs on cloud TPU/GPU via simple decorators, handling packaging, container building, remote execution, and result return.
items.reasoning: Significant new ML tooling announcement from Keras creator. Addresses real pain point of cloud accelerator access. Detailed technical description. Good engagement (14K views). Comparable to Modal but with TPU support.

  • Packages your function, local code, and data dependencies

items.title:
Most people write worse code than AI does.

I've worked in absolutely horrible codebases, 100% writt...
items.content:
Most people write worse code than AI does.

I've worked in absolutely horrible codebases, 100% written and messed up by humans.

So why do we complain about AI-generated slop code today?

Because of the scale at which we are producing it.

Before, you needed a bad programmer to manually write and deploy a ton of bad code.

Today, you can generate virtually unlimited bad code very cheaply and without any constraints.

So the quality of the code might be improving, but the overall amount of technical debt is increasing exponentially.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama argues that while AI may write better code than many humans, the real problem is the scale of bad code production - unlimited cheap generation creates exponentially more technical debt.
items.reasoning: Insightful framing of the AI code quality problem. High engagement (395 likes, 27.5K views). Moves beyond simplistic 'AI code is bad' takes to the systemic scale issue. Santiago is a well-followed AI educator.

items.title: LMAO. Last year most of Silicon Valley was predicting AGI in 2027 (which, spoiler alert, ain’t gonna...
items.content:
LMAO. Last year most of Silicon Valley was predicting AGI in 2027 (which, spoiler alert, ain’t gonna happen).

Now the most prominent former advocate of AGI in 2027, @DKokotajlo, says 2029, and Groks writes it up as “AI forecasters shorten timelines for AGI”, when on net Daniel has moved his mean projection back by two years.

@eli_lifland is as 2033, but  the headline kind of obscures that, too.

Even @grok knows that panic sells; nuance doesn’t.

But ...
items.content:
Folks, I gave a cute example of a hallucination earlier today because I thought it was funny. 

But if you think hallucinations are remotely solved (as some people alleged in the comments), you really need to look at this recent Stanford study, in which recent models *completely confabulate visual materials they have never even seen.*

items.title: agents can do everything*, which causes a UX problem, your users have vastly different experiences, ...
items.content:
agents can do everything*, which causes a UX problem, your users have vastly different experiences, some better and some worse

thinking like an RPG can help here

when you start an RPG game, you start with one weapon, and you learn to fight with it. as you progress, you pick up and learn new weapons. and by the final boss, you can wield all the weapons to defeat him. very rewarding

if you'd started the game with all the weapons, it would be too overwhelming, you wouldn't know which weapon to learn first, and you'd probably stop

this isn't a new idea, but i've used a few tools recently that reminded me of this latter experience

*not everything of course, but you get the point
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: Yohei Nakajima uses RPG progression as a metaphor for agent UX design - suggesting agents should gradually reveal capabilities rather than overwhelming users with all features at once.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful UX insight from BabyAGI creator about progressive disclosure in agent design. Moderate engagement, original analogy applied to a real problem in agent products.

thinking like an RPG can help here

when you start an RPG game, you start with one weapon, and you learn to fight with it. as you progress, you pick up and learn new weapons. and by the final boss, you can wield all the weapons to defeat him. very rewarding

if you'd started the game with all the weapons, it would be too overwhelming, you wouldn't know which weapon to learn first, and you'd probably stop

this isn't a new idea, but i've used a few tools recently that reminded me of this latter experience

*not everything of course, but you get the point

items.title:
It’s nice gig making LLMs.

You can distribute free models that suck.

Hype them to the skies.

Take...
items.content:
It’s nice gig making LLMs.

You can distribute free models that suck.

Hype them to the skies.

Take credit for the hundreds of millions of user you’ve got.

And when they make a mistake, you can blame the users for not using the paid models.

items.title: JAX is what a well-designed low-level machine learning framework looks like. Good design lets you de...
items.content: JAX is what a well-designed low-level machine learning framework looks like. Good design lets you deliver much greater performance with much lower effort. Bad design is the exact opposite.

items.title:
this is soooo wrong.

the whole point of AGI is to be *general*, across all problems. that’s literal...
items.content:
this is soooo wrong.

the whole point of AGI is to be *general*, across all problems. that’s literally what the G stands for, general.

Olympiad problems are narrow, and don’t per se have anything special to do with AGI. 

For some discussion of AGI definitions, see @hendrycks et al (including me and @Yoshua_Bengio) at https://t.co/bPvZogQSaj

items.title: @skbpf as i have said a million times, their unreliability is a serious issue in many mission critic...
items.content:
@skbpf as i have said a million times, their unreliability is a serious issue in many mission critical applications.

you can call that “probabilistic” but the problem remains.

and it’s a bit of an abuse of the term. they aren’t calculating and reporting probabilities.

and they didn’t say “i am not confident that Mamdani is not mayor” with some p value.

you wouldn’t use a calculator that was “probabilistic” in the way in which you use that word, and you shouldn’t plan a war or do people’s taxes that way, either.

@skbpf as i have said a million times, their unreliability is a serious issue in many mission critical applications.

you can call that “probabilistic” but the problem remains.

and it’s a bit of an abuse of the term. they aren’t calculating and reporting probabilities.

you wouldn’t use a calculator that was “probabilistic” in the way in which you use that word, and you shouldn’t plan a war or do people’s taxes that way, either.

items.title: I think it's as unimaginable in the mind of someone who has thousands of people on payroll why you "...
items.content:
I think it's as unimaginable in the mind of someone who has thousands of people on payroll why you "wouldn't" just hire a second person

As it it unimaginable to me why you "would"

I personally love my life without managing people, without calls and meetings, without interpersonal drama, team retreats, company conferences, having to fly everywhere to meet people, and I still make a lot of money

I think it's really a blindspot of status quo "we've always hired so why not?"

On reverse you could of course say me operating solo forever is a blind spot too. Could I make much more money hiring people? Maybe yes but then I'd lose my very chill and very free life I have now that's worth more to me than money

items.content:
OpenAI has acquired TBPN, a livestreaming tech talk show complete with suits and gongs. 

Could definitely imagine AI influencers or other AI podcasters - not just builders like Peter Steinberger, the creator of OpenClaw - getting acquired by AI labs or partnering with them as well. 

"Media" and where education and influence take place is much broader today than it was pre-ChatGPT. Just look at what Rundown AI has been able to pull off with their newsletter alone. 

Worth it to note that the majority of comments on the YouTube stream announcement are either negative or congratulating them on making money.

items.content:
@g_br_l For me it's:
- I deploy in 0 seconds (edit live code) instead of 3 seconds (laptop -> GitHub -> webhook -> server pull)
- I have access to live db, much easier than messing around with stale db copies
- I can work on my laptop or phone via SSH anywhere, when I close my laptop or phone it doesn't stop running, also I can switch between them easily because Claude Code runs on my server

items.title:
I really like this articulation of shift from behavioral → decision traces. 

We're building this at...
items.content:
I really like this articulation of shift from behavioral → decision traces. 

We're building this at Arize because every agent can have its own feedback loop.

Here's how your teams can tactically make that shift: 

1. Collect the traces — this is the highest-value signal. Trace everything possible. Capture every step the agent took - every piece of context gathered, the escalations, the tool calls, etc. Traces are powerful because they capture why decisions are made.

2. Eval the outcome & the trajectory. The evals on the outcomes verify did the decision work. It's ultimately what matters, but it misses how the agent got there.  

Eval the trajectory — this is the critical unlock.

This reveals intent, loopholes, and failure modes the outcome misses. Because you have traces, you don’t just see what happened, you see how the decision evolved. 

3. Feed signal back — use these signals to improve the agent.  The reasoning can share whether the agent needed more context, tools and prompts that need improvement, etc.
items.author: @aparnadhinak
items.summary: Aparna Dhinakaran (Arize AI) outlines a framework for shifting from behavioral evaluation to decision traces in AI agent observability: collect traces, evaluate both outcomes and trajectories, and feed signals back for improvement.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical framework for AI agent evaluation from a domain expert (Arize AI CEO). Good engagement and actionable content about agent observability practices.

items.content_html:

We're building this at Arize because every agent can have its own feedback loop.

Here's how your teams can tactically make that shift:

1. Collect the traces — this is the highest-value signal. Trace everything possible. Capture every step the agent took - every piece of context gathered, the escalations, the tool calls, etc. Traces are powerful because they capture why decisions are made.

2. Eval the outcome & the trajectory. The evals on the outcomes verify did the decision work. It's ultimately what matters, but it misses how the agent got there.

Eval the trajectory — this is the critical unlock.

This reveals intent, loopholes, and failure modes the outcome misses. Because you have traces, you don’t just see what happened, you see how the decision evolved.

3. Feed signal back — use these signals to improve the agent. The reasoning can share whether the agent needed more context, tools and prompts that need improvement, etc.

items.title:
The most complete Claude Code setup ever built is now free. 

27 agents. 64 skills. 33 commands. All...
items.content:
The most complete Claude Code setup ever built is now free. 

27 agents. 64 skills. 33 commands. All open-source. 

It started as a hackathon project. Ten months of daily use turned it into a full operating system for AI coding.

The agents handle planning, code review, build fixes, and security audits. 

The skills cover test-driven development, token optimization, and memory persistence. 

The commands let you trigger any workflow with a slash.

It ships with AgentShield built in:

> 1,282 security tests included
> 98% test coverage out of box
> 60% documented cost reduction
> Works across four major tools

It runs on Cursor, OpenCode, and Codex CLI too. Not just configs or dotfiles. 

This is agents, hooks, rules, and MCP servers wired together.

One repo replaces weeks of setup.

items.title: I just helped hundreds of business professionals build their first AI agents in my AI Agent Mastermi...
items.content:
I just helped hundreds of business professionals build their first AI agents in my AI Agent Mastermind. 

Here are five built by people who had never opened a terminal before this week:

1️⃣ A personalized morning brief. She took my original morning brief setup, remixed it with global news, local news from Sweden, a Linear ticket queue, a joke of the day for the family breakfast table, and a motivation quote based on business needs.

2️⃣ Someone who had never touched a command window used PowerShell and natural language to solve a screenshot clipping issue they had been working around for months.

3️⃣ A Spanish learning app called Vamanos, built on a "carefully negotiated token schedule" so it does not eat into her consulting work. Cultural context profiles for herself and her husband, token alerts so Claude can parent her if she goes off the rails, and Easter eggs with advice from my course sprinkled throughout. She built it for a trip she is taking next year. Sorry Duolingo.

4️⃣ A Google Commute Agent. Built because Google Calendar has no built-in way to book meetings back to back with distance awareness. It scans for events with physical addresses, calculates real drive time via Google Maps API, and automatically adds commute and parking buffer blocks around each one.

5️⃣ A weekly research briefing delivered to Gmail, connected to Perplexity for live web research, with a clickable macOS desktop app for whenever she wants to run it outside the schedule.

These are all solving problems that had been annoying someone for months or years, or ways to show up more fully in their lives.

It’s weirdly addictive to watch. 

We’re opening a second cohort soon. Enrollment will be extremely limited, and it’ll only be open to people on the waitlist.

Join the waitlist here: https://t.co/UK2jqJT2Jj

If a new model shares a feature with a trusted model, that area probably doesn't need scrutiny. 

Mo...
items.content:
If a new model shares a feature with a trusted model, that area probably doesn't need scrutiny. 

Model diffing isolates the features unique to the new model—where new risks are most likely to be located.

LiteParse Samples 📄🧑‍🏫

LiteParse is our free and fast document parser that can parse text with boun...
items.content:
LiteParse Samples 📄🧑‍🏫

LiteParse is our free and fast document parser that can parse text with bounding boxes from any document. I’ve created a new repository that contains some demos on how you can make use of its outputs!
✅ Comparison against PyPDF and PyMuPDF
✅ Visual Citations: search for any keyword and see joined bounding boxes pop up in the source images

It should be a default tool to help AI agents OCR any document type (supports PDF, Word, Powerpoint + dozens of other formats), before heavier-weight VLM-based document parsing solutions (like our own LlamaParse).

LiteParse Samples repo: https://t.co/eoICBmvEnq
LiteParse: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

items.title: Back in the Apple II days people would call up our BBS and drop off some files of ASCII art. No GPUs...
items.content:
Back in the Apple II days people would call up our BBS and drop off some files of ASCII art. No GPUs back then.

If you told me that someday I would be able to view color scenes mapped out in depth like this on a device I held in my hand I would have told you to pass the joint so I could get stoned too.

Happy Friday!

Imagine where humanity will be in 40 years.

Our Grandkids might find this message and say “imagine that, Grandpa used to have to have a device in his hand.”

It might be a note stuck in some historical museum they discovered just by using their minds.

NVIDIA is already investing in about a dozen brain/computer interface companies we can’t name unless you are one of the few who look through my neuroscience list here on X.

The future is so weird.

But then it always was.

Doug Engelbart told me he got fired from the R&D lab back in the early 1970s where he invented the mouse because all he could think about was what would happen when everyone got a supercomputer in their hands.

He then pulled out a pair of gloves while he was showing me around his house and said someday everyone would use those instead of a mouse.

At GTC I saw a pair that lets you teleoperate a humanoid robot better than his could.

To all the dream builders. Love.

Keep bringing the weird. Everyone else will catch up someday.

items.title:
You can now make Claude Code and Codex talk with one terminal. 

smux is a tmux setup that lets Clau...
items.content:
You can now make Claude Code and Codex talk with one terminal. 

smux is a tmux setup that lets Claude Code and Codex read, type, and trigger keys across panes. 

That means two tools can pass work back and forth, reply inside the same workspace, and collaborate without APIs.

It works by turning tmux into the common layer.

Each pane becomes a place the model can inspect and control through bash, using built-in automation and a bridge CLI.

In practice, this opens a simpler multi-agent stack.
> Pair code review with execution
> Split planning from implementation
> Hand off debugging live

The terminal stops being just your interface. It becomes theirs too. 

100% open-source.

items.title:
@random_walker @BasilHalperin Thanks for the engagement and thoughtful questions, Arvind!

1) Yes, t...
items.content:
@random_walker @BasilHalperin Thanks for the engagement and thoughtful questions, Arvind!

1) Yes, the intention was to clearly distinguish AI capability from diffusion, and the scenario descriptions and questions are consistent with this.

2) Per the above, the rapid scenario description is intended to mean that AI is *as capable as* "highly competent CEOs" at the relevant tasks--it does not mean to say anything about social preferences. So, it is left to forecasters to think about what that capability would lead to in terms of actual economic impacts. Underlying views about rates of diffusion are likely a key factor driving forecasts and are mentioned in many rationales.

3) This is a great question and something we'll take a closer look at. We don't have preexisting analysis directly on this point, but it is a type of analysis we should be able to do. Hope to have more to say soon!
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI researcher responds to questions about their AI forecasting methodology, clarifying the distinction between AI capability and diffusion, and how rapid scenario descriptions relate to AI matching 'highly competent CEO' capabilities without implying social preferences.
items.reasoning: Niche academic discussion about AI forecasting methodology. Low engagement but substantive methodological clarification relevant to AI economic impact forecasting.

2) Per the above, the rapid scenario description is intended to mean that AI is *as capable as* "highly competent CEOs" at the relevant tasks--it does not mean to say anything about social preferences. So, it is left to forecasters to think about what that capability would lead to in terms of actual economic impacts. Underlying views about rates of diffusion are likely a key factor driving forecasts and are mentioned in many rationales.

3) This is a great question and something we'll take a closer look at. We don't have preexisting analysis directly on this point, but it is a type of analysis we should be able to do. Hope to have more to say soon!

items.title: The human body can only be pushed so far, and I think a lot of us are pushing ourselves too far, me ...
items.content:
The human body can only be pushed so far, and I think a lot of us are pushing ourselves too far, me included. 

We do need to worry about:
1. Sleep and exercise
2. Eating right
3. Getting off of our devices
4. Having analog experiences

This AI world has sped up our lives and put a lot of pressure on us to keep our agents busy. Something on my mind this Friday afternoon.

items.title: @flutterwhat Oh, that's not the only one that exists. NVIDIA showed me one running underneath their ...
items.content:
@flutterwhat Oh, that's not the only one that exists. NVIDIA showed me one running underneath their desk that simulated the world from a single picture and a single prompt, and it was fucking badass. 

Google does have a lot of advantages in the world, and I expect them to be showing off some really badass things in October when they bring out their Orion glasses. They have more video data than anybody else—well, yeah, even more than ByteDance has—but there will be others, like I said. And there are others already. 

But it doesn't matter; the world is about to go all world model. I've seen:
1. Video games run by world models running on a laptop
2. Robots controlled by world models
3. Autonomous cars controlled by world models

It is going to be a world model world soon.

items.content: It's been a very frustrating and unproductive week for me. I tried to do several of my projects the "right" way (as opposed to just hacking them until they work), but I re-discovered how utterly fragile and unreliable most of the tech tools we have are once you try to use them even slightly outside of the beaten path of what they were designed to handle in terms of the tech stack/use cases. Oh well, there is always the next week. :/

What stood out:

1⃣ GPU starvation is usually a data problem, not a model problem. He walked through...
items.content:
What stood out:

1⃣ GPU starvation is usually a data problem, not a model problem. He walked through cases where utilization was stuck around 15 to 20 percent, and the fix had nothing to do with architecture changes and everything to do with getting data onto the GPU fast enough.
items.author: @mlopscommunity
items.summary: Key finding: GPU starvation is usually a data problem, not a model problem. Cases where GPU utilization was 15-20% were fixed by improving data feeding, not architecture changes.

items.title:
Building on PokeeClaw to prepare for Saturday's hackathon

I'm a judge on tomorrow's PokeeClaw Hacka...
items.content:
Building on PokeeClaw to prepare for Saturday's hackathon

I'm a judge on tomorrow's PokeeClaw Hackathon @Pokee_AI so I'm building a few things on it to prepare.

First step?

Can I sign in?

Instant. This isn't the pain that setting up a MacMini with OpenClaw represents.

Second, can I do something useful? 

I had it make me a list of all the AI communities on Reddit, since I want to build a version of my https://t.co/zleMXMaSi9 site, which watches the whole AI community here on X and finds interesting news, papers, models, and lots of other posts here on X.

Took a couple of minutes. 

Then a couple more minutes I had a custom news feed from Reddit of interesting AI posts there. 

What is PokeeClaw? It isn't a chatbot.

PokeeClaw is a zero-setup, enterprise-secure AI agent that works 24/7 across your apps and the web to plan, execute, and automate real work. Without fragile local setups or Mac minis.

PokeeClaw works like OpenClaw, but with stronger security, 70% lower token usage, and zero setup. It runs in a secure sandbox architecture with isolated environments, approval workflows, role-based access control, and audit trails built in—giving teams an enterprise-ready AI agent that can execute real work across apps and the web.

If it's this easy to build a web page with interesting AI content on it (less than 10 minutes of work) I'm expecting to see some amazing things on Saturday. 

Try it yourself at https://t.co/YRNXMyP06F

items.title: The idea of a virtual world has been stepped on, pissed on, and further abused than any other concep...
items.content:
The idea of a virtual world has been stepped on, pissed on, and further abused than any other concept I've seen in recent history. But the dream still survives.

What is the dream? 

The dream is that we can make reality better. 

We can add the names of the stars that we see above our heads to the stars. We can add the names of the mountains around us to the mountains. We can add the names of the trees and the plants around us to the trees and the plants.

But why stop with reality? Soon we're going to be faced with a new kind of virtual reality, one that I call the Holodeck (Tony told me he also calls it the Holodeck), an AI-generated 3D experience that changes reality itself. This is a hard concept to sell to human beings who seem attached to their realities, but I've been in the labs. I've seen the magic:

1. The magic of going to Coachella and putting on a pair of headphones that improves the audio coming off of the performances at that music festival. I've already done that; very few other people have.
2. The magic of learning anything on top of the device you're trying to fix. I've already done that in Germany. Most people haven't.

And there is plenty of other magic that I just don't have time to detail today. I've written two books about spatial computing, but even those didn't get all the magic.

The idea is that we can improve ourselves, see things that were unseeable before, and entertain ourselves. 

We could see the entire Formula One track, even if you're sitting in the rich people's seats like I did in Abu Dhabi a few weeks ago. 

Some of us have this dream where we can get friends, whether they're close or far, to our poker game on a Friday night and have everybody included. 

Or that we could go to Disneyland and take the grandparent who can't walk anymore along on the experience so that they could spend more time with their grandkids. 

The dream is that we could open any book and see not just the words, but the meaning behind the words, the author's own thinking, and maybe even a virtual world that springs out of the book where we could experience what the author truly wanted us to experience. 

We dream of a new world where we get superpowers. 

But there are so many naysayers and so many pointy-haired bosses who just don't understand that the dream has been put inside a box on the shelf in the closet. 

But Tony, God bless his heart, pulled the box off the shelf and looked at it afresh. 

And for that, I love him.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer writes a lengthy essay about the enduring dream of virtual/augmented reality, covering spatial computing, world models, AI-generated 3D experiences, and the 'Holodeck' concept. Mentions personal experiences at Coachella, Formula One, and with various AR/VR demos.
items.reasoning: Substantial personal essay from a well-known tech commentator about the convergence of AI and spatial computing. Moderate engagement but very long-form and more visionary than technical.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer writes a lengthy essay about the enduring dream of virtual/augmented reality, covering spatial computing, world models, AI-generated 3D experiences, and the 'Holodeck' concept. Mentions personal experiences at Coachella, Formula One, and with various AR/VR demos.
items.content_html:
The idea of a virtual world has been stepped on, pissed on, and further abused than any other concept I've seen in recent history. But the dream still survives.

What is the dream?

The dream is that we can make reality better.

We can add the names of the stars that we see above our heads to the stars. We can add the names of the mountains around us to the mountains. We can add the names of the trees and the plants around us to the trees and the plants.

But why stop with reality? Soon we're going to be faced with a new kind of virtual reality, one that I call the Holodeck (Tony told me he also calls it the Holodeck), an AI-generated 3D experience that changes reality itself. This is a hard concept to sell to human beings who seem attached to their realities, but I've been in the labs. I've seen the magic:

1. The magic of going to Coachella and putting on a pair of headphones that improves the audio coming off of the performances at that music festival. I've already done that; very few other people have.

2. The magic of learning anything on top of the device you're trying to fix. I've already done that in Germany. Most people haven't.

And there is plenty of other magic that I just don't have time to detail today. I've written two books about spatial computing, but even those didn't get all the magic.

The idea is that we can improve ourselves, see things that were unseeable before, and entertain ourselves.

We could see the entire Formula One track, even if you're sitting in the rich people's seats like I did in Abu Dhabi a few weeks ago.

Some of us have this dream where we can get friends, whether they're close or far, to our poker game on a Friday night and have everybody included.

Or that we could go to Disneyland and take the grandparent who can't walk anymore along on the experience so that they could spend more time with their grandkids.

The dream is that we could open any book and see not just the words, but the meaning behind the words, the author's own thinking, and maybe even a virtual world that springs out of the book where we could experience what the author truly wanted us to experience.

We dream of a new world where we get superpowers.

But there are so many naysayers and so many pointy-haired bosses who just don't understand that the dream has been put inside a box on the shelf in the closet.

But Tony, God bless his heart, pulled the box off the shelf and looked at it afresh.

And for that, I love him.

items.title:
@paramendra @tbpn My number is public: +1-425-205-1921

Now that I've gotten far enough along to bui...
items.content:
@paramendra @tbpn My number is public: +1-425-205-1921

Now that I've gotten far enough along to build this: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

I see the problem.

1. Tokens cost money. Site costs about $200 a day to run on tokens.
2. API calls cost money. Site costs about $300 a day to run on API calls.

So, the biggest gating factor is capital. I can't afford to burn hundreds of dollars every day forever (and any ideas we would come up with would cost more).

So am now working on automating the rest of my life to be able to connect money into the system faster and take care of customers. 

And building a business plan to take to investors. But I have enough vision to do that. 

The other problem is that Elon eventually will get there. So any business plan has to take on what happens when the billionaires figure it out?  And they eventually will.

items.title: What I hate about copyright and trademark law is that you're essentially forced by the law to send l...
items.content:
What I hate about copyright and trademark law is that you're essentially forced by the law to send legal letters, takedown requests and eventually sue

If you don't, whatever rights you own are invalidated in court whenever you do really need to defend them by the judge because the infringer can prove "you didn't defend your rights earlier either"

Which absolutely does not work in 2026 because by sueing someone smaller than you in the internet era they will absolutely leverage that to the maximum possible for marketing purposes

"The big guy is sueing us underdogs"

Which I would do too if I was the underdog

And now everyone knows about your new competitor while before they didn't!

Aka Streissand effect

items.title:
The breakthrough is not one bigger model.

It is multiple agents with clear roles:

→ one designs th...
items.content:
The breakthrough is not one bigger model.

It is multiple agents with clear roles:

→ one designs the research steps
→ one uses real tools and databases
→ one checks the work to catch errors
→ one writes conclusions, but only when evidence supports them

In other words, less “guess and generate,” more “test and prove.”

Build AI-Enhanced Web Apps: https://t.co/YkL2dtkJSf
———
Amazon Summary:

"This book shows you step-by-step and example-by-example how to build sites and applications that take advantage of large language models (LLMs) like GPT, Claude, and Llama. Written especially for web developers comfortable with React or Next.js, this book introduces the tools and techniques you need to add sophisticated AI features like Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), document summarization, chatbots, and more to your web-based projects.

"It guides you through AI development using only JavaScript and other common web dev skills–no Python or Machine Learning experience required. You’ll learn by working with full-scale AI projects that solve actual business problems. You’ll soon be delivering user-friendly, efficient interfaces that make the absolute best use of AI tech."

items.title: My takeaway: the next wave of AI value will come from systems that can do disciplined work, not just...
items.content:
My takeaway: the next wave of AI value will come from systems that can do disciplined work, not just fluent work.

That is why self-running research is such an important signal.

Watch the video to see how this works in practice, then tell me what you think .

Are we looking at the future of scientific discovery, or do you think human-led research will remain the real bottleneck?

items.title:
That changes what AI can actually do in science.

Instead of relying only on training data, it can c...
items.content:
That changes what AI can actually do in science.

Instead of relying only on training data, it can connect to sources like PubMed and biological databases, then iterate through real research workflows.

The result:

→ screen thousands of candidates
→ design potential therapies
→ produce lab-ready outputs
→ validate findings experimentally

This is where AI stops being an assistant and starts becoming an operator.

items.title: @AlexFinn I remember telling people the exact same thing about social media, and people would line u...
items.content:
@AlexFinn I remember telling people the exact same thing about social media, and people would line up to tell me how I was wrong. You and I both know this to be true. 

The truth is: just keep building. They will all figure it out in their own time. In the meantime, you will have a stack of OpenClaws, Hermes, or whatever else, and you will be running and doing interesting things that they will all have to pay for. 

If they want to try to catch up, they are going to be way behind. This is the cost for not valuing innovation and keeping up. Most people don't, and that is just the way it is. 

In the meantime, I am watching and learning. Thank you.

items.title: @MrVladimirX The world really does change when a lot of people have glasses. We'll be able to join 3...
items.content:
@MrVladimirX The world really does change when a lot of people have glasses. We'll be able to join 3D spaces to play mini golf, have new kinds of business meetings, or get friends together to play blackjack in someone's house without actually being there.

I remember a researcher at Microsoft who used this technology to put email and calendar screens all around him. If he needed to check his schedule, he just had to turn around and look at that specific screen while keeping his main work screen in front of him. It allowed him to stay focused and remain in a flow state longer than usual. 

He even had speakers set up around his office for spatial notifications:
1. If an email needed attention, it would lightly chime behind him.
2. If something like Slack or Discord (though they didn't exist back then) interrupted him, it would chime over on the left.
It was a really interesting system.

If you go to the Sphere in Las Vegas, it’s essentially a giant VR headset for 4,000 people to experience together. That’s very cool, but it would be even better if all 4,000 people could have that experience from their own point of view and actually interact with things. That is the promise of VR. 

The current issues are:
1. The devices are too heavy and uncomfortable to wear for long periods.
2. They separate you from the real world.
3. The cost is still too high. Even in Silicon Valley, $3,500 is too much for most people to afford.

Because of those factors, nobody really has them yet, so group experiences haven't evolved the way they eventually will. We’re still a little way off from that future, but we know where it’s going because we’ve seen glimpses of it. I’ve been in multi-party VR experiences where it felt like magic to have your family all shooting aliens together. 

There is a lot of magic locked up in this technology. We just need the devices to get a lot better before we can truly reach that world.

@Elon_Orbit @AlexFinn You missed the crowds in China where people are signing up to get their open club up and running, and you missed the many millions of people who have one. 

I saw that at NVIDIA's GTC where people stood in line to do the same. It's happening. It might not be mainstream yet, but revolutions always start small.

Explore a world of knowledge with free online cours...
items.content:
MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning!  @MIT

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month:      

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode

https://t.co/kY22MxdmuV

items.content:
We hosted our first ever @llama_index in-office meetup yesterday to pregame First Thursdays 🦙🔥

There’s more involved events we’ll be putting on with panels/talks/workshops where you learn stuff, but this one is pure vibes and fun. We had alcohol and also many non-alcoholic beverage options 🍻🧃

If you couldn’t make it (we had hundreds of registrants but didn’t admit too many initially to be conservative about space), we’re going to be throwing this every month!

Next time we will double the number of pizzas. I will be putting on my EDM playlist 🪩

items.title:
AGI 

AI systems like GPT, can pass advanced standardized tests e.g., the LSAT, GRE, Medical exams a...
items.content:
AGI 

AI systems like GPT, can pass advanced standardized tests e.g., the LSAT, GRE, Medical exams at a level comparable to Ivy League graduate intelligence. In future to embed GPT with biosynthetic intelligence and for a silicon chip to truly match an Ivy League graduate’s intelligence, it would likely need to incorporate neuromorphic computing (hardware mimicking the brain’s structure) or brain-inspired architectures capable of reasoning, learning, and generalizing knowledge beyond narrow tasks.

Notably, AI systems have demonstrated superhuman capabilities in complex games, strategic planning, and certain cognitive tasks.

A significant milestone was achieved by DeepMind’s AlphaGo Zero, an AI program that mastered the game of Go without human data. Starting from scratch, AlphaGo Zero surpassed the performance of previous versions, including those trained on human games, within a matter of days. This accomplishment underscores AI’s potential to develop expertise exceeding that of human professionals in specialized areas.

In the realm of strategic decision-making, AI has also demonstrated superior performance. A study analyzing over 5.8 million move decisions made by professional Go players over 70 years found that human decision-making improved following the advent of superhuman AI. The research suggests that exposure to AI prompted human players to explore novel strategies, enhancing their performance.

Furthermore, AI’s rapid development has led to discussions about its potential to match or exceed human intelligence across various fields. Surveys of AI experts have indicated a belief that there is a 50% chance of AI outperforming humans in all tasks within the next few decades.

items.summary: Long post about AGI discussing GPT passing standardized tests, neuromorphic computing, AlphaGo Zero, and expert surveys on AI timeline.
items.reasoning: Recycled AGI talking points from early 2025 references. Not timely or original. Hashtag-heavy.
items.summary_html: Long post about AGI discussing GPT passing standardized tests, neuromorphic computing, AlphaGo Zero, and expert surveys on AI timeline.
items.content_html:
AGI

AI systems like GPT, can pass advanced standardized tests e.g., the LSAT, GRE, Medical exams at a level comparable to Ivy League graduate intelligence. In future to embed GPT with biosynthetic intelligence and for a silicon chip to truly match an Ivy League graduate’s intelligence, it would likely need to incorporate neuromorphic computing (hardware mimicking the brain’s structure) or brain-inspired architectures capable of reasoning, learning, and generalizing knowledge beyond narrow tasks.

Notably, AI systems have demonstrated superhuman capabilities in complex games, strategic planning, and certain cognitive tasks.

A significant milestone was achieved by DeepMind’s AlphaGo Zero, an AI program that mastered the game of Go without human data. Starting from scratch, AlphaGo Zero surpassed the performance of previous versions, including those trained on human games, within a matter of days. This accomplishment underscores AI’s potential to develop expertise exceeding that of human professionals in specialized areas.

In the realm of strategic decision-making, AI has also demonstrated superior performance. A study analyzing over 5.8 million move decisions made by professional Go players over 70 years found that human decision-making improved following the advent of superhuman AI. The research suggests that exposure to AI prompted human players to explore novel strategies, enhancing their performance.

Furthermore, AI’s rapid development has led to discussions about its potential to match or exceed human intelligence across various fields. Surveys of AI experts have indicated a belief that there is a 50% chance of AI outperforming humans in all tasks within the next few decades.

items.content:
@lower_votingage @DKokotajlo to be clear, i think it is great that he updates his estimates regularly based on new data. all scientists should do that.

my irritation is with the media that hypes up the early predictions and delta forward and pays less attention to the later ones and when the delta goes backwards.

items.title: @kate_rouch I'm in the cheering section of your audience, cheering you on. Myself and many family me...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Supportive message to someone dealing with cancer diagnosis.
items.reasoning: Personal/emotional, not AI-related.
items.summary_html: Supportive message to someone dealing with cancer diagnosis.
items.content_html:
@kate_rouch I'm in the cheering section of your audience, cheering you on. Myself and many family members have gone through this, and it does take a lot of courage. I wish you all the best.

I can't wait until you come back and say you beat cancer. I hope you share some of the journey because there's a lot of people out here who are suffering the same way and are trying to figure out their path through life. Having somebody like you showing people how to do it with grace and dignity and take on the hard days will help many people.

Thank you.

items.title:
Why do people elect leaders whose mere appearance suggests a lack of leadership or intellect?

I und...
items.content:
Why do people elect leaders whose mere appearance suggests a lack of leadership or intellect?

I understand that voters might not all be very smart, but seeing leadership in a person's posture and face has been innate for generations.

When times are hard and the future is clouded, isn't it simply self-preservation to want to elect real leaders? 

Canadians have quickly switched from a clown to a leader-like person when they sensed something was going wrong. Why not the Brits or Germans?
